title: Emergency Care: My Cat's Eye is Bulging Out of the Socket
meta_desc: If your cat's eyeball is bulging (proptosis), it is a critical emergency. Learn the causes, immediate steps, and why triage photos are essential.
content_html:
What is Proptosis?

A bulging eye, medically known as proptosis or exophthalmos, occurs when the eyeball is displaced forward from the socket. This condition is often caused by blunt-force trauma, such as a fall, a vehicle accident, or a physical altercation with another animal. In some cases, it may be caused by abscesses or tumors behind the eye pushing the globe forward.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-level medical emergency. If your cat’s eye is bulging, you must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. The displaced eye can lead to optic nerve damage, permanent blindness, or the death of eye tissue due to lack of blood flow. This condition is extremely painful and requires professional medical intervention to manage pain and potentially save the globe.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of the affected eye to your veterinary team can be life-saving. A photo allows the veterinarian to assess the severity of the displacement, identify if the eyelid is trapped behind the globe, and check for signs of rupture. This visual information helps the clinic prepare for your arrival, ensuring the right specialists and surgical tools are ready the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can I push the eye back into the socket myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to push the eye back in. You may cause irreversible damage to the optic nerve, rupture the globe, or cause your cat intense pain that could lead to a bite or scratch.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat calm in a carrier. If possible, gently place a clean, damp, room-temperature cloth over the eye to keep the surface moist, but do not apply any pressure.

faqs.question: Will my cat lose their eye?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the severity of the trauma and how quickly the cat receives care. In many cases of feline proptosis, the eye may need to be surgically removed (enucleation) to prevent chronic pain and infection.
